speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about a fight between two soccer clubs, Lechia and a Jewish club, Maccabi. And you had suggested that the fight that broke out after the game was rooted in anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no question in my mind it was, because Jewish people that play with Lechia. Even older spectators, the public, what did it come to watch, and they wanted Lechia to win. Fortunately, Maccabi won. They were a better club.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you participating in that game?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were playing in the game?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just there. I watched the game, because I didn't belong to that team.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened at the end of the game.

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of the game, when Maccabi won, they started to go into dressing room, and they start the-- kick mostly the public-- the public what they wanted Lechia to win. And they start to fight with the people. There were a lot of people from lodge that come to participate the game. And They did fight. As a matter of fact, I remember one took out a revolver and shot in the air. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the audience-- were the people fighting yelling anything? Were there any negative statements being yelled at Maccabi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, they tried to get out as fast as possible, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because no matter what it is, somebody that got hurt, and they tried to hurt as many as they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any other Jewish organizations besides that sports club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did belong to Ha-No'ar ha-Ziyyoni.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of an organization is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zionists. That was a Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your involvement in the activities that you would engage in with that organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did come there. We had some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kind of activities like from-- I remember we went in 1937, we went to a camp for four weeks with that organization. And then in 1938, we went again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we spend there-- there come some people in that time from Israel. I remember one man, [? Katz, ?] he was from Katowice. He gave some speeches. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a Zionist--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was your intention to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody from the young people, they knew Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was, if I'm remembering correctly, that wasn't my intention, because from my brother's first marriage, all the brothers, they went to Palestine, even the Polacks. They said your place is not here. Go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that that you were talking about from his first marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Nojech.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was married prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Annie-- ?] Hana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kosherowski. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One little girl. I had the picture from that little girl. She was six years old when-- when-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when they took her away with the mother. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] liquidate the ghetto in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The whole ghetto was liquidated in 1942 or 194-- that was already the little ghetto was liquidate in 1942 or 1943, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that little girl's name, your brother's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh. Oh, dear. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I know, but I just now it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: If it comes back to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maryla. [? Marylka. ?] We called her [? Marylka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of your other brothers besides Cluwa, your sister, or Nojech, your brother, were any of your other siblings married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naftula was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hana. Hana. Also Hana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Isenberg. ?] No. They didn't have any children. That's right, [? Isenberg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your best friend before the war, as a teenager, let's say? Anybody come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is so hard to describe. They all were good friends. We all like-- like brothers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a picture there-- there's one, Nisek Wertajm, like I said, he was knowing-- everybody did like him. There is not a person in the world, even being in camp. Even the Germans liked him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about Nisek that was so endearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a person that everybody did like him. He was a real human being. If he could help somebody, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he help you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: He started to learn tailoring in our place and when we were in the first camp, and we didn't know anything about-- everybody try to take some money with them, hide some money, and take it to the camp. And we didn't know. We were very naive. And it happens, he did talk and came over in camp and he said-- I remember he said, I borrow you $10. [INAUDIBLE] is whatever the-- if you're going to live, you're going to-- and it happens after the war, he didn't survive. After the war-- he got married during the war, and we gave back to his wife the $10. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Sometime it come collapse in play. He was like a representative from everybody. He was a very good player, soccer. So he did play even in the Polish teams in the combination from Tomaszow-- all Tomaszow team against the other one. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have some very, very, very good friend. As a matter of fact, next week we have a wedding in New York. I'm going to a friend's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend in Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: A public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish secular school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were Jewish kids in this school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the school set up for Jewish kids or did it just happen that the community that the school was located in was predominantly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Jewish kids. I don't recall what it was set up, but mostly Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Gentile students at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grades of school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till grade 6.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailoring.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I start tailoring, I-- I start when I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would have been 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you started tailoring, you stopped school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I still go to-- went to school when came and I did tailoring.

speaker: interviewer
text: For who?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time is my brother took it over. We had already expand-- make it bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nojech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a good teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody had to teach them by themself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you learning at the family shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it nearby your family home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not very far. It was in the what it called the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the family business, was on that street?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, he got married, and he moved the shop to his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family home, where was the family home located? The name of the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become a full-fledged tailor prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For who were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nojech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a good working relationship with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you dating girls at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any particular girl prior to 1939 that you were particularly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --interested in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you speak of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] What can I say? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just unfortunately. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elbaum. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in Germany when Hitler came to power in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did. We knew. And nobody could believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your understanding in 1933-- you were 15 years old-- what was your understanding of who this man was and what he represented?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know very much. The only thing I remember, we were-- were in a play at that time, and they said that Hitler-- 1933 when [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kaiser Wilhelm or whatever it was, that he became Chancellor, that's all. Something like this. But the rest, nobody did believe that something like this could ever happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he'd done to the Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you talking about during the war people couldn't believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, because in that time, not right in 1933, a few years later, he's sent out Jewish people from Germany. But they did come from Poland. So a lot of people did come to Poland from Germany-- Jewish people at that time-- 1937, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall-- can you speak to the contact that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a family that come over. Their name was Markowic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was in Germany before the war, and he married to a German woman. And there-- and they have two children, a daughter and a son. The son's name was Julius. And I think in 1937 or in '38, they was-- they sent him out-- they had family in Tomaszow. And they did come. And then his wife-- she was German, real German-- and she did come to Tomaszow too. She didn't want to leave them alone. And then when they liquidate the ghetto and everything, she marched together when we were in the transport by train-- till the train, she marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1937, when they came to Tomaszow, they had come why? What had happened in Germany that resulted in them having to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they-- they sent out all the Jews for they were not born in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have contact directly with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --this man, Markowic? What did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius, I even saw a picture. He had a bar mitzvah in Germany-- I remember correctly-- with the tallit and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he tell you about what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that they tried-- they didn't talk very much, but they just-- they had to leave. And the sister went back to Germany as a Gentile. And it happens after the war, I was in Berlin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an Oranienburger Strasse, there was the Jewish Congress. And I recognize the Mrs. Markowic that sit there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And she did cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you know about the rise of Nazism and its implications for the Jewish people before the war-- 1935, 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know very much. The only thing we knew what's going on in this, but we could never ever believe that something like this happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in 1935, the concentration camps, the murdering of Jews hadn't begun large-scale yet. But the Nazis had outlined a little bit of their platform. What did you know in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what the word Nazi meant or what a Nazi was in 1935?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. The only thing we knew, Hitler. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that name represent to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he is a very, very bad man. That's all. We didn't know. Maybe some older people or what, they knew more than I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an understanding of the threat that Hitler potentially posed to Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I remember was a boxing game in Hamburg, and there was a Jewish boxer from Poland. His name was Rotholz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was in 19-- I think in 1937 or '38. I think in '38. The Polish team went to Hamburg, and somehow they shout the team Hamburg [INAUDIBLE] and he did see it the way they knocked out the windows in Hamburg from Jewish stores and so on. And when they went on the ring, and he said I'm going to teach you now, to that German what he did box against him. This I remember correctly. And he did gave-- he knocked him out, with blood. He said, you're going to pay with blood. And in that time, the Polish paper-- the newspaper, they said that he didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] express himself nice on the ring. That's all. That's all that I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: That the-- that Rotholz, the Jewish boxer, had not expressed himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the Polish newspaper they would say I think like this. After all, this is sport, but he and-- the coach, the Polish coach, he took them away on the side and went back to the hotel, not to see so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever listen to radio and hear any speeches by Hitler from Germany or any news from what was going on in Germany before 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could hear, but I didn't pay very much attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any discussion amongst your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of discussion?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it was going on, the-- the killing, they're [SIGHS] abusing Jewish people in Germany. That's all what I know. As a young boy, nobody ever did-- I wouldn't say nobody, but I didn't pay very much attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much the anti-Semitism in your city before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an increase in anti-Semitism as you got closer to 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I remember I had an account, a bank account at it called [INAUDIBLE] Polish-- in the post office there. And near the war, I went up and wanted-- we had to stay in the line, and a lot of people there, they want to withdraw some money. And there was an organization, a Polish youth organization and it call OZON, O-Z-O-N, OZON. This is youth follow, like I would say, like probably like the Hitlerjugend. And they would stay in the line and will have the Jewish people want to go near the window to draw some money, they special blocked it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to the outbreak of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to September 1st.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that organization-- did they wear uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you trying to withdraw money or was your family member-- were family members trying?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did try.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were trying.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my own account. Not very much, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened as you were waiting in line? What took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went once in the morning, right in the morning, and withdraw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they weren't there at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember we went in the old country, everybody like a tailor, like me, had to go three years in the school at night time in order to finish as a, you know, if I want to open a shop, I have to finish a school. After the school, you're going out, you could see Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young people. They'd stay with paint with brushes, going to brush your-- with paint up to-- that was very common.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what year that was? 1938. That was always.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no difference in that kind of anti-Semitic behavior as you got closer to the war? You're saying that that could have taken place in 1930 as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Always. That was always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of September 1st, 1939, the first day of World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it wasn't-- I was in bed still asleep, and all of a sudden, I heard something. I said, what's the matter? And we didn't know. We didn't expect this. And we got up and said maybe it's raining or it start thunderstorm and that's all. And then not very long they said it's a fire not very far where we used to live. What is it? There were two bombs right there that-- and a big-- what they did make materials, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they bombed there. That was the first bombs there. It was about 5:00 in the morning. And the name of that firm was Markowic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made good materials.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the firm that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bombed. Markowic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that by an air strike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Air strike, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any discussion amongst your family of leaving Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time when it has started bomb, and the third day, everybody started to escape, to go. Where are you going? To Warsaw. That was the biggest mistake. And on the way to Warsaw, that calls a small town, Rawa, that was a slaughter house at that time. And the fourth day, say I'm going to Warsaw too. I took my bicycle and ride to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in Warsaw we had-- I didn't know where to sleep, where to go. We didn't have anything to eat. But somehow we got some help from-- and I slept, I remember, in a very small little synagogue-- very small.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just going to have to stop you there. We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
